participants.extracted: Children and infants from rural villages in Udaipur district, Rajasthan, India, drawn from Seva Mandir’s catchment area. The main household sample included children aged 1–3 years at end point (aged 0–5 years at baseline households were censused and 30 households per village were sampled), with a final analysed sample of 2,188 children from 2,898 households across 134 villages: 860 in control villages, 379 in intervention A villages, and 382 in intervention B villages. A spillover sample also included 265 children in villages neighbouring intervention A villages and 302 children in villages neighbouring intervention B villages. The study population was impoverished and largely tribal, with low baseline immunisation rates.

interventions.extracted: The study compared two village-level interventions in rural Rajasthan, India. Intervention A (“immunisation camps”) provided regular, well publicised monthly immunisation camps in villages using a mobile team with a nurse and assistant hired by Seva Mandir. The team held clinics on a fixed date and time each month, and a social worker informed families and educated mothers about the benefits of immunisation. Intervention B used the same immunisation camp infrastructure as intervention A, but also added small incentives for parents: 1 kg of raw lentils for each immunisation administered and a set of thalis (metal plates) when a child completed the full immunisation schedule. Children in these villages received the government’s WHO/Unicef extended package of immunisations, including BCG, three doses of DPT, three doses of oral polio vaccine, and one dose of measles vaccine.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure immunisation uptake in children aged 1–3 years at end point after about 18 months. The main household-survey outcomes were: probability of receiving at least one immunisation (excluding oral polio vaccine), presence of the BCG scar, number of immunisations received, and probability of receiving the complete extended package of immunisation. In the camp administrative data, the planned outcomes were: number of immunisations received among children who had received at least one immunisation in the camp, and probability of receiving all vaccines in the extended package of immunisation. The study also planned complementary analysis of the probability of being immunised in neighbouring villages, including relative risks, at the same end point.
